coredata.dc:title: Investigation of optical, electrical and electrochemical properties of polyaniline/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Sinha, Sayantan
Devi, Naorem Aruna
Nongthombam, Sumitra
Bhujel, Rabina
Rai, Sadhna
Sarkar, Goutam
Swain, Bibhu P.
coredata.dc:description: In the present study polyaniline (PANI)/reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/Ag2O nanocomposites with varying Ag content were synthesized using a facile chemical rout and investigated for the energy storage application. XRD results reveal crystallite size and lattice strain varied from 40.30 nm to 50.02 nm and 0.00117 to 0.00745 respectively. The Ag content was varied from 0.08 at.% to 0.20 at.% as confirmed by the XPS. The carbon network study in Raman and XPS show C-H(B), C-N(B), C-N(Q), C-C(Q), C=N(Q), C=C(Q), and C-C(B) functional groups. The cyclic voltammetry showed the specific capacitances for PRGAg60, PRGAg80, and PRGAg160 nanocomposites are 733.2 Fg−1, 897.9 Fg−1, and 556.8 Fg−1 respectively at 5 mV s−1 scan rate, which indicates that the specific capacitance increases first with Ag loading and then it decreases with farther increase in Ag. Moreover, PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite exhibited good cyclic stability of 87.1% after 5000 charge-discharge cycles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PANI/rGO/Ag2O Nanocomposites
Supercapacitor
Cyclic voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite by an in-situ oxidative polymerization method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Centre for Materials Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Competence Centre for Materials Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to Centre for Materials Science and Technology, Sikkim Manipal Institute of Technology, Sikkim, India for providing electrochemical characterization facilities. Authors also acknowledge University Science Instrumentation Centre, North Bengal University, West Bengal, India for providing SEM facility. Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology Manipur, Manipur, India is also acknowledged for XRD and FTIR instrument facilities. Authors also would like to thank Dr. L. Herojit Singh, Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Manipur for the UV–visible spectroscopy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of PRGAg60, PRGAg80, PRGAg120, PRGAg160, and rGO. PRGAg120(b) and PRGAg160(b) show high magnification images of PRGAg120 and PRGAg160 respectively.
(a) XRD patterns of rGO, pure PANI, PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites.
(b) Williamson-Hall plot for PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites.
FTIR spectra of rGO, PANI and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites.
(a) Raman spectra of rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites.
(b) Deconvoluted peaks of the Raman spectra rGO, PANI/rGO, and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites in the range of 960 cm−1 to 1800 cm−1.
(c) Deconvolution of the second-order Raman peak for spectra rGO, PANI/rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites in the range of 2200 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1.
(a) UV–vis spectra of rGO, PANI, PANI/rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites.
(b) Tauc plots of PRGAg160 and PRGAg60.
(a) Sheet Conductivity versus AgNO3 wt%.
(b) Two probe I-V characteristic for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160.
(a) Broad scan XPS spectra of the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites.
(b) Deconvolution of core orbital XPS spectra of N(1 s) for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160.
(c) Deconvolution of core orbital XPS spectra of O(1 s) for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160.
(d) Deconvolution of core orbital XPS spectra of C(1 s) for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160.
(e) Deconvolution of core orbital XPS spectra of Ag(3d) for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160.
(f) Valance band XPS spectra of PRGAg60 and PRGAg160.
Predicted energy band diagram for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160.
(a) CV curves of rGO, pure PANI, and PANI/rGO/Ag2O composites along.
(b) Galvanometric charge-discharge (GCD) characteristics of rGO, PANI, PANI/rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites at 0.5, 1, 2, and 5 Ag−1 current densities.
(c) Variation in specific capacitance for PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites with AgNO3 wt%.
(d) Bode plot for the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites.
(e) Nyquist plot of the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data for the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites. In the inset the enlarged view at high frequency region.
(f) Cyclic stability: specific capacitance versus cycle number.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite using in-situ polymerization.
Comparison of supercapacitive performance of PANI/rGO/Ag electrode material.
The specific capacitance values obtained from the GCD curves at different current densities for different nanocomposites.
Values of Re, RCT, and C for different nanocomposites, calculated from Nyquist plot.

head.ce:title: Investigation of optical, electrical and electrochemical properties of polyaniline/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Manipur, Langol, Imphal West 795004, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Manipur, Langol, Imphal West, 795004

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Materials Science and Technology, Sikkim Manipal Institute of Technology, Sikkim Manipal University, 737136, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Materials Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Materials Science and Technology, Sikkim Manipal Institute of Technology, Sikkim Manipal University, 737136

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University Science Instrumentation Centre, North Bengal University, West Bengal 734013, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University Science Instrumentation Centre, North Bengal University, West Bengal, 734013

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study polyaniline (PANI)/reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/Ag2O nanocomposites with varying Ag content were synthesized using a facile chemical rout and investigated for the energy storage application. XRD results reveal crystallite size and lattice strain varied from 40.30 nm to 50.02 nm and 0.00117 to 0.00745 respectively. The Ag content was varied from 0.08 at.% to 0.20 at.% as confirmed by the XPS. The carbon network study in Raman and XPS show C-H(B), C-N(B), C-N(Q), C-C(Q), C=N(Q), C=C(Q), and C-C(B) functional groups. The cyclic voltammetry showed the specific capacitances for PRGAg60, PRGAg80, and PRGAg160 nanocomposites are 733.2 Fg−1, 897.9 Fg−1, and 556.8 Fg−1 respectively at 5 mV s−1 scan rate, which indicates that the specific capacitance increases first with Ag loading and then it decreases with farther increase in Ag. Moreover, PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite exhibited good cyclic stability of 87.1% after 5000 charge-discharge cycles.

ce:section.ce:para:
The hybrid supercapacitors (SC) shows the excellent charge storage capability by facilitating both the electrical double-layer capacitor (EDLC) and pseudocapacitor in a single package. To build the hybrid SC, the carbon structures such as graphene, carbon nanotubes, carbon aerogels, etc. with a high ionic-adsorption capability are utilized as the EDLC part, whereas conducting polymers such as polyaniline (PANI), PEDOT, polypyrrole and the d-block metal oxides (MO) are used to form the pseudocapacitance part. To achieve better hybrid SCs ternary composites such as conducting polymer/rGO/MO nanocomposite, each component to overcoming the following issues which may arise:
(i)
With the constant repetition of a redox reaction, the conducting polymers degrade after a few cycles of charge-discharge.
(ii)
MOs have poor conductivity and due to the different functionalities attached to different metals affects the specific capacitance differently.
(iii)
The conductivity and the optical band gap of electrode materials are also affected by the amount of oxygen content in the ternary composite.

Thus, to tune supercapacitive properties by using the MO as electrode materials with different oxygen content, different metals such as Fe [1], Cu [2], Ru [3], Mn [4], etc. have been studied. Among the various conducting polymers and the metal oxides, the incorporation of PANI and AgxO into the rGO network enhances the specific capacitance due to the good electrical conductivity, chemical stability, and fast redox-reaction. Sawangphruk et al. fabricated a high-performance supercapacitor with capacitance 828 Fg−1 measured at a current density of 1.5 Ag−1 based on PANI/rGO/AgNP coated on flexible carbon fiber paper [5]. Dhibar et al. synthesized the PANI/multiwalled carbon nanotube/AgNP nanocomposite with observed electrical conductivity of 4.24 S cm−1 at room temperature and the highest specific capacitance of 528 Fg−1 at 5 mV s−1 scan rate for high-performance supercapacitor electrode [6]. Dhibar et al. reported electrochemical performances of PANI/rGO/AgNP in different electrolytes. The maximum specific capacitance of 591 Fg−1 has been achieved for the nanocomposite at 5 mV s−1 scan rate in 1 M KCl electrolyte [7]. Pattananuwat et al. synthesized solid-state supercapacitor devices by using the one-pot method of AgO/PANI/rGO composites and the reported capacitance was 365.14 Fg−1 at 0.5 Ag−1 current density with capacitive retention of 88% after 4000 charge-discharge cycles at 1 Ag−1 [8]. Usman et al. proposed Ag2O/PANI/rGO nanocomposite for enhanced supercapacitor electrode and observed a specific capacitance of 1572 Fg−1 at 0.05 Ag−1 and 85% capacitance retention after 3000 charge-discharge cycles [9]. Although the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite has been investigated for the supercapacitor applications, as per knowledge, very few works have been reported until now. However, the previous studies did not focus on the role of (i) optical bandgap, (ii) various chemical functionalities, and (iii) variation of Ag2O on the supercapacitivity in the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite material for supercapacitor applications.

Therefore, the present work focuses on the followings: (i) to investigate the structural and vibrational properties of PANI/rGO/Ag2O, (ii) to instigate optical and electrical properties of PANI/rGO/Ag2O, and (iii) electrochemical and electronic environments of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites.

GO synthesis was carried out following the modified Hummer's method [10–12]. 3 g of graphitic powder was added to 66 mL of concentrated H2SO4. 1.5 g of sodium nitrate (NaNO3) was added and stirred for 30 min to mix everything well. At this point, the mixture becomes highly dense and black. The temperature was reduced to below 20 °C by putting the beaker into an ice bath and 9 g potassium permanganate (KMnO4) was added very slowly under stirring condition. After that, the temperature was increased to 35 °C and allowed to stir for 2 h. 300 mL deionized (DI) water was added very slowly and the temperature was increased to 98 °C and stirred for another 30 min. A dark orange color mixture is formed indicating the oxidation of graphite. After adding 250 mL DI water 15 mL hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was added to stop the reaction [1,13]. The precipitate was filtered and washed with 10% HCl solution to remove the excess Mn2+ ions. Finally, it was washed several times using DI water and dried in vacuum for 12 h to get GO.

0.3 g GO was finely crushed and dispersed into 150 mL DI water (GO 2 mg/mL concentration) by ultrasonication at room temperature for 1 h. AgNO3 with the varying amount from 0.06 g to 0.16 g was added to the GO dispersion and stirred for 1 h. 3 mL hydrazine hydrate was added and the reaction temperature was increased to 80 °C and stirred for 2 h. It was turned into brownish-black from yellowish-orange indicating the reduction of GO and formation of rGO/Ag2O composite. The product was filtered and washed several times with DI water and finally by acetone. The filtered black substance was dried in air overnight [Scheme 1 ]. Four separate nanocomposites with different amount of AgNO3 such as 60 mg, 80 mg, 120 mg, and 160 mg were prepared and labeled as rGO/Ag60, rGO/Ag80, rGO/Ag120, and rGO/Ag160 respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite by an in-situ oxidative polymerization method
ce:section.ce:para: As prepared rGO/Ag60 to rGO/Ag160 were ultrasonicated for 1 h in 50 mL 1 M HCl to form the dispersion. 2 mL aniline was added to the dispersion and allowed to stir for 1 h. 0.6 g ammonium persulphate (APS) was dissolved in 50 mL 1 M HCl and added dropwise into the dispersion. It was kept under vigorous stirring for 6 h. The color of the solution was changed gradually from light yellow to blue and finally into dark green [Scheme 2 ]. After filtering the green precipitate was washed several times with DI water. Finally washed with ethanol and dried for 12 h to get PRGAg60, PRGAg80, PRGAg120, and PRGAg160 nanocomposites.

The morphology characterization of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites was performed by using a JEOL JSM-IT100 Scanning Electron Microscope with magnification x1000 to x8000. The structure of pure rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites were characterized by Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer with the scan rate of 0.1°/s. The Raman analysis was done with the excitation wavelength of 532 nm at 1.5 mW power (WitecAlpha300 RS) with a step size of 1 cm−1. The PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites were further characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra on PerkinElmer Spectrum Two FT-IR Spectrometer using a light source with wavenumber 400–4000 cm−1. The optical properties of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite were characterized by UV–Visible spectrometer using Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer with a step size of 1 nm within 200 to 1000 nm range of wavelength. The current-voltage (I-V) characteristic was analyzed with the KEYSIGHT B2901A instrument by coating the nanocomposite on the ITO-glass substrate. The composition, chemical bonding, electronic environments of core orbital spectra of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) using VG ESCALABMK II with an Mg(Kα) X-ray (1253.6 eV) source. The base pressure of the XPS chamber was maintained 10−10 Torr and the instrument is normalized with Au(4f) lines with a step size of 0.1 eV. The electrochemical analysis was performed using CHI608E, CH Instruments with the following electrode configuration: working electrode – carbon electrode coated with graphene; counter electrode – Pt wire electrode; reference electrode – Ag/AgCl. The electrolytic solution contained a 1 M H2SO4 solution. The CV was taken by varying the scan rate from 5 mV s−1 to 50 mV s−1 within the potential window −0.2 V to 0.8 V. GCD was also recorded in the same potential window. AC impedance was measured by varying the frequency from 1 MHz to 1 Hz with an amplitude of 5 mV. The specific capacitance (C
sp ) from the obtained CV curves was calculated by employing the formula:
(1)
C sp
=
∫ Current
∆ V × m × Scan rate
where ΔV is the potential window, m is the mass of the active electrode material, and the numerator was calculated by integrating the corresponding CV curve.

Fig. 1 shows the morphology of the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites with different Ag content. The SEM images show the spherical Ag2O particles are distributed unevenly in the multiphase cloud-like structure of PANI/rGO. Furthermore, the high magnification images of PRGAg120 and PRGAg160 nanocomposites show the size of the spherical Ag2O particles varies from 159 nm to 210 nm.

Fig. 2(a) shows the XRD of pure rGO, PANI and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites with different Ag content. The XRD planes of rGO were observed at 2θ = 24.4° which corresponds to the (002) plane [11] and the interplanar spacing (d) in rGO was calculated as 0.36 nm. The smaller d value suggests the reduction of the oxygen functionalities bonded in the GO layers [14]. The diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ = 25.35°, 20.4°, and 14.95° correspond to three intense planes of (200), (020) and (011) respectively for PANI [15]. The rGO peaks were almost suppressed in the PANI/rGO/Ag2O composites indicating the proper intercalation of PANI between the rGO layers. Moreover, five sharp peaks were observed at 2θ = 27.88°, 32.3°, 46.36°, 54.9°, and 67.63° which correspond to (110), (111), (211), (220), and (222) diffraction planes of silver oxide (Ag2O) respectively (JCPDS No. 76-1393) [16]. The crystalline size was determined from the Debye-Scherrer's formula,
(2)
D =
kλ
β cos θ
where, k = instrument constant = 0.9, λ = wavelength of the X-ray (1.5418 Å), β = full width at half maxima (FWHM) for the most intense peak of the XRD spectra, θ is the half of the position of the most intense peak. The crystallite size decreased from 50.02 nm to 40.1 nm with increasing Ag content. The lattice strain of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites was calculated from the Williamson-Hall (WH) plot shown in Fig. 2(b). The formula associated with WH plot is:
(3)
β cos θ =
kλ D
+ 4 ϵ sin θ
where ε is the strain, D is the crystallite size, k is the instrumental constant and β is the full width at half maxima (FWHM). The lattice strain of the nanocomposites increases from 0.00394 to 0.00745 with the increase of Ag content.

Fig. 3 shows vibrational signatures of various chemical bonding in the pure rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite with different Ag content. The FTIR spectrum of rGO shows peaks at wavenumbers 1069, 1556 and 2329 cm−1 which assigned to the chemical bonding vibrations of C-OH stretching, CC stretching, and CO stretching respectively [5]. After the addition of rGO in the PANI, the CH stretching peak gets sharper in comparison to that of pure PANI which might be due to the interaction between PANI and rGO. With the addition of PANI and silver oxide in rGO, seven new chemical-bonds were observed at 798, 1034, 1293, 1473, 1568, 2341, and 2982 cm−1 corresponding to CH out-plane bending, CH in-plane bending, CN stretching for the benzenoid ring, CC stretching for the quinoid ring, CC stretching for the benzenoid ring, CO, and CH stretching respectively indicating the presence of PANI in the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites [9]. The presence of all the functional groups confirmed the successful in-situ polymerization process.

Fig. 4(a) shows that Raman spectra of rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O with different Ag content in the range of 500–3500 cm−1. The first-order Raman peaks for pure rGO centered at 1207, 1273, 1343, 1470, and 1586 cm−1 are assigned to the Raman bands D1, D2, D, D3, and G respectively [11]. The D band appears due to the defects and disorder of rGO caused by the oxygen and carbon functionalities in the surface of the rGO and the appearance of the G band is due to the in-plane vibration of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms of rGO. In PANI/rGO the first-order Raman peaks observed at 1177, 1198, 1280, 1375, 1481, 1549, and 1598 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching vibration for the benzenoid ring, CN stretching vibration for the benzenoid ring, CN stretching vibration for the quinoid ring, CC stretching vibration for the quinoid ring, CN stretching vibration for the quinoid ring, CC stretching vibration for the quinoid ring, and CC stretching vibration for benzenoid ring respectively [5].

Fig. 4(b) shows the deconvoluted peaks of the Raman spectra for rGO, PANI/rGO, and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites in the range of 960 cm−1 to 1800 cm−1. The first-order Raman peak for rGO was deconvoluted into five Gaussian peaks centered at 1207.4, 1272.7, 1343.3, 1469.8, and 1585.9 cm−1 corresponding to the Raman bands D1, D2, D, D3, and G respectively which is in good agreement with the previous work reported by Rajendar et al. [17]. The intensity ratio between D and G band i.e. ID/IG indicates the ratio between the sp3 disorder and the ordered sp2. The rGO shows ID/IG = 1.16 which shows a less amount of defect-density in the rGO. In case of rGO/PANI, the first order peak was deconvoluted into seven Gaussian peaks centered at 1177.2, 1198.5, 1280.2, 1375.9, 1481.5, 1549.5, and 1597.8 cm−1 which corresponds to CH stretching vibration for the benzenoid ring, CN stretching vibration for the benzenoid ring, CN stretching vibration for the quinoid ring, CC stretching vibration for the quinoid ring, CN stretching vibration for the quinoid ring, CC stretching vibration for the quinoid ring, and CC stretching vibration for benzenoid ring respectively.

Fig. 4(c) shows the deconvolution of the second-order Raman peak for spectra rGO, PANI/rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites in the range of 2200 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1. The second-order peaks of rGO were located at 2314.1 cm−1, 2472.2 cm−1, 2642.6 cm−1, 2837.9 cm−1, and 3200.5 cm−1 attributed to 2D1, 2D2, 2D, (D + G), and 2G respectively. The 2D band is the second harmonic of the D band which measures the rGO stacking order and the (D + G) arises due to the resonance of the longitudinal optical (LO) phonon modes of D and G bands [18]. The second-order Raman peak of PANI/rGO nanocomposite was deconvoluted into seven peaks located at 2322.5 cm−1, 2504.1 cm−1, 2559.6 cm−1, 2720.0 cm−1, 2933.2 cm−1, 2933.3 cm−1, 4055.8 cm−1 assigning to 2C-H(B), 2C-N(B), 2C-N(Q), 2C-C(Q), 2C=N(Q), 2C=C(Q), and 2C-C(B) respectively.

Fig. 5(a) shows the UV–vis spectra of rGO, PANI, PANI/rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O within the wavelength of 190–1000 nm range. Three prominent absorption peaks associated with PANI located at 353 nm, 439 nm, and 789 nm were observed due to the π-π* transition, polaron-π* transition and π-polaron transition [11]. In PANI/rGO/Ag2O two peak-shifts concerning those of PANI/rGO are observed from 353 nm to 367 nm and from 789 nm to 799 nm indicating the interaction of Ag2O nanoparticles with the PANI/rGO network [19]. The UV–vis spectrum of rGO shows a peak at 241 nm which corresponds to π-π* transition of CC bond in the rGO network [12].

Fig. 5(b) and (c) shows the Tauc plots of PRGAg160 and PRGAg60 nanocomposites which gives the relation of hν versus (αhν)r. The bandgap (Eg) of PRGAg160 and PRGAg60 nanocomposites was calculated by the Tauc's formula
(4)
αhν = B
hν −
E g
r
where, B is the proportionality constant, h is the Plank constant, α is the absorption coefficient, ν is the frequency of the light, and r = 1/2 for indirect bandgap material and r = 2 for direct bandgap material. A good linear fit was obtained for the direct bandgap of PRGAg160 and PRGAg60 from the Tauc plots depicted in Fig. 5(b) and (c). The band gaps were calculated from the intercepting point of the x-axis (y = 0) and the extrapolated straight lines of linear fit regions. The measured band gaps were 2.27 eV and 2.58 eV for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160 respectively.

Fig. 6(a) shows the dependence of four-probe electrical conductivity vs. AgNO3 wt% used in the synthesis of the PANI/rGO/Ag2O composites pallets. The result indicates that the conductivity monotonically decreases from 2.98 S cm−1 to 0.69 S cm−1 with an increase in the loading of Ag2O. This is because of the addition of less conductive metal-oxide to the PANI/rGO network. Fig. 6(b) shows the two probe I-V characteristics of PRGAg60, PRGAg80, PRGAg120, PRGAg160 and PRGAg60, nanocomposite. All I-V characteristics showed Ohmic nature the conductance of PRGAg60, PRGAg80, PRGAg120, PRGAg160, and PRGAg60 are 1.11954, 0.94063, 0.37388 and 0.36079 (Ohm-cm)−1 respectively. This result reveals that Ag2O incorporated PANI/rGO played a dominating roll in the current transport mechanism through the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite.

Fig. 7(a) shows the broad scan of the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites. The binding energy of C(1s), Ag(3d), N(1s), and O(1s) core orbitals are found at 283.3 eV, 366.6 eV, 399.2 eV, and 529.5 eV respectively, which conforms with the works of Bhujel et al. and Usman et al. [9,22]. The atomic composition of the PANI/rGO/Ag nanocomposite was estimated by Eq. (5).
(5)
X i
=
A i
S i
∑
A i
S i
where A
i is the area under the core orbital, S
i is the surface sensitivity factor of the elements present [23]. The surface sensitivity factor of the C(1s), Ag(3d), N(1s), and O(1s) are 0.314, 0.499, 0.733, and 6.277 respectively. The atomic percentage of C, N, O, and Ag are 82.69, 4.97, 12.27 and 0.08 at.% respectively for PRGAg60 nanocomposites and those for PRGAg160 nanocomposite are 79.83, 10.88, 9.09, and 0.20 at.% respectively.

Fig. 7(b) shows the deconvolution of core orbital spectra of N(1s) for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160 nanocomposites to evaluate the oxidation states/functional group of nitrogen exist in the composites, where five Gaussian peaks according to the probability of coordination number and electronegativity of the elements present in the nanocomposites. The binding energy at 397.6 eV, 398.99 eV, 399.5 eV, 401.6 eV, and 406.23 eV are observed for nitrogen attached with a silver (NAg), nitrogen atom attached with a carbon atom, (NC), nitrogen atom attached with nitrogen, (NN), nitrogen atom attached with an oxygen atom (NO), and nitrogen atom double-bonded with oxygen, (N=O) respectively. In the PRGAg60, the peaks due to NAg, NC, NN, NO, and NO appeared at binding energies 397.1 eV, 298.98 eV, 400.37 eV, 403 eV, and 406.3 eV respectively. The FWHM of the NAg, NC, NN, NO, and NO for PRGAg160 nanocomposite is 0.581, 1.203, 2.311, 4.589, and 1.633 respectively and those for PRGAg60 nanocomposite are 0.489, 1.720, 2.727, 1.482, and 0.628 respectively. The areal ratio of NAg, NC, NO and NO with NN in the PRGAg60 nanocomposite are 0.015, 1.696, 0.081, 0.0161 respectively and the areal ratio of NAg, NC, NO and NO with NN in the PRGAg160 nanocomposite are 0.0116, 0.429, 0.356, 0.033 respectively.

Fig. 7(c) shows O1s core orbital spectra deconvolution into six Gaussian peaks to attribute various electronic environments in the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites. The binding energies at 530.20 eV, 530.85 eV, 532.1 eV, 533.62 eV, 534.72, and 534.86 eV corresponds to O-C-C, O-C=C, O-N-C, O-N-N, O-O-C, and OO bonding respectively. The binding energies of O-C-C, O-C=C, O-N-C, O-N-N, O-O-C, and OO varied to 530.47 eV, 532.14 eV, 533.24 eV, 533.88 eV, 534.76 eV, and 536.29 eV respectively with Ag content varied from 0.2 at.% to 0.08 at.%. The FWHM of the O-C-C, O-C=C, O-N-C, O-N-N, O-O-C in the PRGAg60 nanocomposite are 1.242, 1.427, 2.011, 1.311, 3.529, and 0.861 respectively and those in the PRGAg160 are 1.424, 1.783, 0.971, 1.410, 2.696, and 0.620 respectively. The areal ratio of O-C-C, O-C=C, O-N-C, O-N-N, and O-O-C with OO in the PRGAg60and PRGAg160 nanocomposites are 52.705, 40.313, 35.333, 76.356, 61.182 and 5.891, 17.469, 34.860, 9.305, 6.601respectively.

Fig. 7(d) shows the deconvolution of the C1s spectrum of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites. The binding energies of CAg, CC, CC, CN, CO, and CO varied from 283.8 eV, 284.5 eV, 285.2 eV, 285.9 eV, 287.98 eV, and 288.7 eV to 284.56 eV, 284.85 eV, 285.91 eV, 286.64 eV, 287.43 eV, and 288.46 eV with Ag content varied from 0.2 at.% to 0.08 at.% respectively. These results agree with the previous work of Nongthombam et al. with slight changes [13]. The FWHM of the CAg, CC, CC, CN, CO, and CO is 0.988, 1.237, 1.181, 2.288, and 5.394 respectively. The areal ratio of CAg, CC, CN, CO, and CO with CC in the PRGAg60 and PRGAg160 nanocomposites are7.375, 1.142, 1.437, 1.560 and 1.724, 0.156, 1.462, 1.141 respectively.

Fig. 7(e) shows the XPS spectra of Ag in the PRGAg nanocomposites where two peaks are obtained at 367.1 eV (Ag3d5/2) and 373.1 eV (Ag3d3/2) for the PRGAg160 nanocomposite and for PRGAg60 nanocomposite, the peaks are obtained at 367.5 eV (Ag3d5/2) and 373.3 eV (Ag3d3/2) of the binding energy. These results agree with the work of Usman et al. [9]. The splitting of 3d doublet was 6 eV for PRGAg160 and 5.8 eV for PRGAg60 which indicates the formation of Ag nanoparticles [19]. The FWHM of the Ag3d5/2 and Ag3d3/2 in the PRGAg60 and PRGAg160 nanocomposites are 2.654 eV, 3.358 eV and1.972 eV, 1.824 eV respectively. The intensity ratio of Ag3d5/2 to Ag3d3/2 in the PRGAg60 and PRGAg160 nanocomposites are 0.906 and 0.984 respectively.

Fig. 7(f) shows the valence band spectra obtained from XPS for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160 nanocomposites. As the figure shows the gaps between the valence band (VB) and the Fermi level are 0.302 eV and 0.241 eV for PRGAg160 and PRGAg60 nanocomposites respectively. Ef denotes the Fermi energy level. Comparing with the bandgap data obtained from Tauc plot, the energy gaps between the conduction band (CB) and the Fermi level are 2.029 eV and 2.278 eV for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160 respectively. This reveals that the Fermi level is closer to the VB compare to the CB indicating the p-type nature of the nanomaterials [24]. Peaks 1 and 2 in Fig. 7(f) appears due to the 2p σ-electrons of the CC structure. Peak 3, 4, 5, and 6 may be attributed to the sp3, sp2, s2p2, and sp. hybridized electrons respectively. Peaks 7 and 8 were predicted to be coming from 2s orbital electrons. Fig. 8 shows the predicted band diagram for PRGAg60 and PRGAg160.

Fig. 9(a) shows the CV curves with scan rate varying from 5 mV s−1 to 50 mV s−1 for pure PANI, rGO, PANI/rGO, and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites. The CV curves obtained for the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites show the duck-shaped voltammogram consisting of oxidation-reduction peaks indicating the redox reaction in PANI and Ag2O along with the ionic adsorption in rGO. The mass of the active material loaded into the glassy carbon working electrode was 0.1 mg in all cases. The maximum specific capacitances were obtained at a 5 mV s−1 scan rate for pure rGO, PANI, and PANI/rGO as 73.7, 293.8, and 549 F g−1 respectively. The PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites achieved the highest specific capacitance at 5 mV s−1 as 733.2, 897.9, 790.6 and 556.8 F g−1 for PRGAg60, PRGAg80, PRGAg120, and PRGAg160 respectively. Fig. 9(b) shows the galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) characteristics of rGO, PANI, PANI/rGO and PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites at 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 Ag−1 current densities. From the GCD curves, the calculated specific capacitances for rGO, PANI, PANI/rGO PRGAg60, PRGAg80, PRGAg120, and PRGAg160 were 45.6, 23.5, 141.3, 255.7, 297.5, 209.8, and 47.1 F g−1 respectively at 0.5 Ag−1 current density. The specific capacitances obtained from different current densities are listed in Table 2. Fig. 9(c) shows the variation of specific capacitance in different scan rates with respect to the AgNO3 wt% used in the synthesis of PANI/rGO/Ag2O composites, which shows the highest specific capacitance of 897 Fg−1 for PRGAg80 nanocomposite at the scan rate of 5 mV s−1. In every scan rate, the specific capacitance increases from PRGAg60 to PRGAg80 but after that, it decreases. Although the increase of the Ag2O part in the PANI/rGO/Ag2O composite increases the pseudocapacitance, at the same time it also decreases the conductivity of the material as found from the conductivity and I-V characterization. Because of this decrease in conductivity, the charge transfer resistance is increased causing a tendency of dropping the specific capacitance. Thus, when the Ag2O is loaded into the PANI/rGO matrix a tread-off is to be done between the possible enhancement in pseudocapacitance and decrease in conductivity; which leads to an optimum level of Ag2O after which the specific capacitance starts decreasing with the addition of Ag2O. The figure also shows that the specific capacitance decreases as the scan rate increases, this happens because in a lower scan rate the electrons get sufficient time to interact with the material surfaces inside the pores of the material and the effective surface that can react with the electrolyte increases. On the other hand, in a high scan rate, the redox reaction happens only between the material surfaces in touch with the electrolyte thus decreases the effective surface area. Fig. 9(d) shows the Bode plot for the PRGAg60 to PRGAg160 nanocomposites. Fig. 9(e) shows the Nyquist plot of the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data for the PRGAg60 to PRGAg160 nanocomposites where negative of the imaginary impedance (−Z″) plotted with real impedance (Z′). In this EIS characterization an a.c. voltage was used having 5 mV amplitude with frequency varying from 1 Hz to 1 MHz. The inset shows the enlarged view of the plots at high frequency. The graph shows a semicircular part in the high-frequency region and a linear part in the low-frequency region. The diameter of the semicircular part denotes the charge transfer resistance (RCT). The smaller diameter of the semicircle indicates smaller charge transfer resistance of the nanocomposite which is preferable for the supercapacitive use of the nanocomposite. The intercept of the curve to the Z′ axis is the impedance imposed by the electrolyte (Re). The capacitance (C) due to the interfacial reaction can be calculated by the following equations from EIS data.
(6)
C =
τ
R CT
(7)
τ =
1
ω max
where τ is the time constant and ωmax is the frequency where the imaginary part of the impedance (Z″) is maximum [2]. The values of Re, RCT, and C calculated from the Nyquist plot are presented in Table 3. The capacitances calculated from the Nyquist plot follows a similar trend as obtained from CV and GCD. Fig. 9(f) shows the cyclic stability of supercapacitance for 5000 charge-discharge cycles. The result shows a good cyclic stability of 87.1% after 5000 cycles at 0.5 Ag−1. A comparison in the supercapacitance performance with various other research groups in terms of electrolyte, specific capacitance, electrode configuration, and cyclic stability are summarized in Table 1 .

The PANI/rGO/Ag2O ternary nanocomposites with varying the Ag content was successfully synthesized using simple in-situ polymerization. The crystallite size ranges between 40.30 nm to 50.02 nm. Various chemical bonding vibrations due to CH out-plane bending, CH in-plane bending, C-N(B), C=C(Q), C=C(B), CO, and CH stretching were observed in the FTIR. The Raman spectrum of pure rGO shows the D and G band at 1343.3 cm−1, and 1585.9 cm−1 respectively with ID/IG = 1.16. The chemical bondings such as C-H(B), C-N(B), C-N(Q), C-C(Q), C=N(Q), C=C(Q), and CC appeared in the Raman spectra of PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposites suggesting the successful formation of PANI. The band-gap increased from 2.27 eV (PRGAg60) and 2.58 eV (PRGAg160) with an increase of the Ag content because Ag2O has a high bandgap. The XPS survey scan shows the C(1s), Ag(3d), N(1s), and O(1s) core orbitals at 283.3 eV, 366.6 eV, 399.2 eV, and 529.5 eV respectively. The at.% of Ag varies from 0.08 at.% (PRGAg60) to 0.2 at.% (PRGAg160). The highest specific capacitance achieved was 897.9 Fg−1 at a 5 mV s−1 scan rate with good cyclic stability of 87.1% after 5000 charge-discharge cycles at 0.5 A g−1. Thus, the PANI/rGO/Ag2O nanocomposite can be suggested as a suitable electrode material for the hybrid supercapacitors.

ce:para.ce:sans-serif: Sayantan Sinha:Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft.Naorem Aruna Devi:Data curation.Sumitra Nongthombam:Visualization, Investigation.Rabina Bhujel:Software, Validation.Sadhna Rai:Writing - review & editing.Goutam Sarkar:Investigation.Bibhu P. Swain:Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Centre for Materials Science and Technology, Sikkim Manipal Institute of Technology, Sikkim, India for providing electrochemical characterization facilities. Authors also acknowledge University Science Instrumentation Centre, North Bengal University, West Bengal, India for providing SEM facility. Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology Manipur, Manipur, India is also acknowledged for XRD and FTIR instrument facilities. Authors also would like to thank Dr. L. Herojit Singh, Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Manipur for the UV–visible spectroscopy.
